title: 'podman exec --tty' and 'podman run --tty' connects the inner stderr to the outer stdout
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

I am not entirely sure about this one, so feel free to correct me if I am wrong.

@HarryMichal and I have been staring at how [Toolbx](https://github.com/containers/toolbox) wires up the standard output and standard error streams via Podman to the final child process running inside the container.  It seems like using the `--tty` flag connects the standard error stream of the inner process to the standard output of the outer.

The command running inside the container can be simplified to this somewhat pathological invocation:
```
$ sh -c 'sh /etc'
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
```

Teasing apart the standard output and output, we see that the error message is being written to the standard error stream:
```
$ sh -c 'sh /etc' 1>/dev/null
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
$ sh -c 'sh /etc' 2>/dev/null
```

When this is run inside a container:
```
$ podman run --rm --interactive --tty fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc'
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
$ podman run --rm --interactive fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc'
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
```

So far so good, but when we separate out the standard output and error streams, we see something odd.  When `--tty` is used the error message seems to be written to the outer `podman` process' standard output:
```
$ podman run --rm --interactive --tty fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc' 1>/dev/null
$ podman run --rm --interactive --tty fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc' 2>/dev/null
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
```

If `--tty` is not used, then the error gets written to the outer `podman` process' standard error as expected:
```
$ podman run --rm --interactive fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc' 1>/dev/null
/etc: /etc: Is a directory
$ podman run --rm --interactive fedora:36 sh -c 'sh /etc' 2>/dev/null
```

Is this expected behaviour?  Or a bug?  :)

I understand that `--tty` should only be used when there's a controlling terminal attached to the outer `podman` process.  I am using the redirected streams only as a way to understand what's exactly going on.

**Describe the results you expected:**

I expected that running with or without `--tty` wouldn't affect the stream to which the error message is getting written.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

API Version:  4.2.1
Go Version:   go1.18.5
Built:        Wed Sep  7 21:58:19 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman` or `brew info podman`):**

```
podman-4.2.1-2.fc36.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

I haven't tried Podman from Git, but I did look at the Troubleshooting Guide.  The guide has an entry for *Unexpected carriage returns are outputted on the terminal* but I don't think this is the same thing.  The presence of a nested terminal can affect some behaviour specific to terminals, like whether to use a carriage return or not, but not the standard output and error streams.  I could be wrong, though.

comments.body: `--tty` does not have a separate STDOUT and STDERR. It puts the terminal in raw mode, and both streams write raw data to it, losing all distinction between the streams. This is unavoidable if using `--tty` or other raw terminal applications.

Oh, yes, of course.  Once you have written to a terminal, it's just a single stream of bytes.  The STDOUT and STDERR distinction is only there before that happens.

Thanks for helping me through the haze, @mheon :)
